story:
A detective's call had promised that a quick meeting would "clear things right up," but Marty wasn't reassured as he drove Maggie toward the city. His hands were sweating, his questions stacking up. Maggie, oddly calm about her ex-husband Dan's death in what the news called a home invasion, stared out the window and hummed. She hadn't cried. She hadn't even teared up. He'd told her they were going to the station. That was the first lie of the morning. He also couldn't stop thinking about Friday night: traffic had kept him crawling, his calls to Maggie had gone unanswered, and the divorce with Dan had been ugly. Alibi, motive, timeline—he knew what detectives would ask; he watched enough true crime to recite the checklist.

When he asked what she'd tell the detectives, Maggie said she'd tell the truth. He nudged: she'd stayed home Friday, right? She chided him for watching too many crime shows. He tried to keep his voice steady. He tried not to think about the thing he'd already discovered: the texts. He'd seen messages that sounded like she'd been meeting Dan, like she might be wondering if there was a way back.

Traffic thinned, fields filled the windows, and Maggie sat up with a start. The station was in the city, but they were far past that now—flat land, no cars. "Where are we?" she asked. Marty wiped his palms on his jeans. "Mags, do you think I'm stupid?" She joked that he was just lost, but the silence thickened.

"How long has it been going on?" he asked, eyes on the road. He didn't wait for a lie. "I read your texts," he said, anger in his voice. "You wanted to get back together." Maggie said it wasn't like that, that they were only catching up. He pressed harder on the gas. She told him to turn around, voice sharpening. He didn't. "I just don't get it," he said. "What did he have that I don't?" When she repeated that it wasn't like that, he cut her off. "That's not what he told me on Friday."

Her breath caught. He'd decided hours ago there would be no station, no interview room. He'd watched enough interrogation videos to know what those rooms did to people. Better to find a quiet place to talk. He told her he'd gone to Dan's house on Friday. He said he'd done it for them.

Maggie's left hand was buried in her jacket pocket. He realized what she was doing a second too late. He grabbed her wrist and yanked out her phone. The screen showed 911—connected. He hurled the phone out the window, his breath stuttering. Sirens rose in the distance anyway.

Color drained from Maggie's face. She hadn't wanted to believe it when a detective visited her at work, when the call came about Dan—not even when she found a small, rust-brown stain on one of Marty's shirts. The police had asked her to help—to keep him talking, to learn why. She had stayed calm for that. She had hummed to keep him calm. She had dialed 911 to catch his words.

Marty clenched the wheel. The weight of what he'd done felt heavier now that it was spoken, and at least the pretending was over. Ahead, the road was empty; behind them, the sirens were coming fast.
